title: Guidelines for Relationship Effectiveness: Keeping the Relationship (GIVE) A way to remember these skills is to remember the word GIVE (DEAR MAN, GIVE): (Be) Gentle

pages.text:
128
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
«
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6   (I. E. Worksheets 4, 5)
Guidelines for Relationship Effectiveness:
Keeping the Relationship (GIVE)
A way to remember these skills is to remember the word GIVE (DEAR MAN, GIVE):
(Be) Gentle
(Act) Interested
Validate
(Use an) Easy manner
(Be)Gentle
Be nice and respectful.
No attacks:
No verbal or physical attacks. No hitting, clenching fists. No harassment of any
kind. Express anger directly with words.
No threats:
If you have to describe painful consequences for not getting what you want,
describe them calmly and without exaggerating.
No “manipulative” statements, no hidden threats. No “I’ll kill myself if you . . . ”
Tolerate a “no.” Stay in the discussion even if it gets painful. Exit gracefully.
No judging:
No moralizing. No “If you were a good person, you would . . . ”
No “You should . . . ” or “You shouldn’t . . . ” Abandon blame.
No sneering: No smirking, eye rolling, sucking teeth. No cutting off or walking away.
No saying, “That’s stupid, don’t be sad,” “I don’t care what you say.”
(Act)Interested
Listen and appear interested in the other person.
Listen to the other person’s point of view.
Face the person; maintain eye contact; lean toward the person rather than
away. Don’t interrupt or talk over the person.
Be sensitive to the person’s wish to have the discussion at a later time. Be
patient.
Validate
With words and actions, show that you understand the other person’s
feelings and thoughts about the situation. See the world from the other person’s
point of view, and then say or act on what you see.
“I realize this is hard for you, and . . . ”, “I see that you are busy, and . . . ”
Go to a private place when the person is uncomfortable talking in a public
place.
(Use an)
Easy manner
Use a little humor.
Smile. Ease the person along. Be light-hearted. Sweet-talk.
Use a “soft sell” over a “hard sell.” Be “political.”
Leave your attitude at the door.
Other ideas:

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Guidelines for Relationship Effectiveness: Keeping the Relationship (GIVE) A way to remember these skills is to remember the word GIVE (DEAR MAN, GIVE): (Be) Gentle
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
